data.title: Nutrien Q1 2025 Earnings: Expect More Near-Term Pressure (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Nutrien Ltd. (NYSE:NTR) just reported its Q1 2025 results and the initial negative market reaction is justified given the somewhat lacklustre results.

At the same time, the stock has been performing exceptionally well in recent months - outperforming both some of its peers and the broader equity market as well. Being a high beta stock, NTR was supposed to fall sharply in recent months as the S&P 500 retraced from its all-time highs, but the opposite has happened.

This performance suggests that the stock has likely reached a bottom in late 2024 when short-term pessimism has resulted in a mismatch between the share price and actual business fundamentals.

There's also increasing probability that the recent bear market in key agricultural commodities is now over and with that the average net selling price of NTR's fertilizers has already hit rock-bottom.

With that in mind, it is important to sift through the quarterly noise from the recent earnings release and then assess Nutrien's current valuation by taking into account ongoing business performance.

A Disappointing Quarter

In a nutshell, the first quarter of 2025 was nothing to be excited about. Nutrien missed consensus estimates, both on the top and bottom line as weather-related delays had a negative impact on Retail sales while potash average net selling price continued to decline in North America.

Full year guidance was reiterated, but that wasn't enough to appease investors who seem to have expected this quarterly release to mark a long-awaited turnaround for company's earnings.

Instead, sales remained at record lows and the company was barely profitable on a GAAP basis, with adjusted earnings per share falling by 76% year-on-year.

Even though the first quarter is not of seasonal importance to the same extent that the second one is, the sharp fall of gross margins to a record-low is foreshadowing profitability headwinds through the rest of the year.

Overall, the quarter was disappointing and the likelihood of NTR share price continuing on its ascent in the coming months has been diminished.

While NTR remains very conservatively priced on a long-term basis, the share price seems to have been running ahead of itself in recent months as the Price/Sales multiple has just recently reached levels last seen in early 2023.

More Near-Term Pressure

As average net selling price in Potash continued to decline and Nutrien also faced cost headwinds from higher natural gas prices, the company's gross margin reached a new low. The drop on a trailing-twelve months is not substantial, if we ignore the Phosphate segment, however it does not bode well for expectations for the rest of 2025.

Higher volumes and lack of certain one-off items within the retail segment are what's expected to support earnings through the rest of 2025 and allow Nutrien's management to achieve their full fiscal year guidance.

2025 full-year guidance ranges have been maintained and reflect expectations for growth in upstream fertilizer volumes, higher downstream Retail earnings and lower capital expenditures.

Source: NTR Q1 2025 Earnings Release

So far, there's nothing to suggest that Nutrien is no-longer a long-term buy, but the share price is likely to face more pressure in the coming months.

Last year, I used the relationship between the company's return on equity and the Price/Book multiple as a reason to justify my rating upgrade. Following the yesterday's earnings release, however, the two variables have now gone in different directions and gap between the two has widened. Therefore, unless there is an exogenous shock that results in higher fertilizer prices, I expect NTR to face more near-term pressure.

Nutrien's free cash flow has also fallen sharply during the last quarter as profitability headwinds were exacerbated by working capital outflows. And while the latter is could reverse through the rest of the year, it seems highly likely that NTR would continue to trade at a free cash flow yield that is significantly below the current yield on U.S. Treasuries.

At the same time, capital expenditure remains very low and is currently below the sanitary minimum of the company's annual depreciation expense. This is not a cause of concern as long as the Capex to Depreciation ratio does not remain below 100% for a longer period of time. Nevertheless, it is a sign that the current level of capital expenditure is unsustainable beyond the short-term.

Conclusion

Nutrien's strategic competitive positioning remains unchanged and with that my initial investment thesis remains unchanged. Having said that, exceptionally strong price performance in recent months, in combination with the disappointing quarterly results are now likely to result in more downward pressure on the stock price over the coming months.

data.title: Franklin California High Yield Municipal Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The municipal bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, posted a slightly negative result for the first quarter of 2025 (Q1). January and February saw positive returns supported by healthy inflows of funds and steady demand, while returns posted in March reversed those gains.
  • Contributors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with no external credit rating, underweight allocation to AA rated bonds and security selection in AA rated bonds.
  • Detractors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity, underweight allocation to muni bonds with two to five years to maturity, underweight allocation to AAA rated bonds and security selection in A rated bonds.
  • Outlook: Potential policy changes under US President Donald Trump’s administration, such as the possible elimination of tax exemptions for certain bonds and the cancellation of federal leases, are adding uncertainty to the muni market outlook, alongside broader concerns about how federal decisions may impact local economies.

Performance Review

The fund’s yield curve positioning detracted from relative performance during the quarter, primarily driven by our overweight to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity and underweight to muni bonds with two to five years to maturity.

Outlook

There was a significant swing in market sentiment across Q1 as participants digested the first few months of the new Trump administration. January saw positive excess returns across a number of sectors as market sentiment moved higher in anticipation of new business friendly initiatives. This was replaced by a negative shift in risk appetite during February and March as unclear tariff policies became the market’s focus. Throughout the quarter, investment-grade munis underperformed US Treasuries. Despite modest outflows in March, muni funds overall benefited from steady inflows earlier in the quarter.

Muni credit fundamentals remain broadly stable, with signs of improvement even in sectors that struggled post-pandemic, though revenue growth is moderating, and economic tailwinds are slowing. High reserves and fiscal flexibility continue to support most issuers, and credit ratings are expected to remain steady over the next six months, though sector-specific challenges persist.

Potential policy changes under the Trump administration, such as the possible elimination of tax exemptions for certain bonds and the cancellation of federal leases, are adding uncertainty to the muni market outlook, alongside broader concerns about how federal decisions may impact local economies. At the same time, US consumer sentiment is softening, and overall uncertainty around the economic growth outlook has increased in response to the administration’s proposed reforms and policy direction.

However, hard data continues to indicate a resilient US economy. The labor market appears to have reached an equilibrium of a low level of both new additions and layoffs, with a still-low unemployment rate, and economic activity remains solid.

Going forward, our economists are looking to deregulation and tax reforms to support growth over the medium term. The heightened uncertainty surrounding the administration’s trade policy and the impact on US businesses makes US Federal Reserve policy difficult to predict. It remains to be seen whether risks are skewed toward a significant slowdown in growth or higher inflation. However, our economists remain optimistic on US growth, considering that the economy remains on solid footing and pro-growth measures are still expected. Consequently, we are penciling in only one 25-basis point rate reduction in 2025, though an absence of rate cuts this year is also possible.

As of 03/31/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 5 star and Class A shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 100, 97 and 77 Muni California Long funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 11/15/2006 and the fund began offering A Class shares on 9/10/2018. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 11/15/2006, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance; for A Class periods prior to 9/10/2018, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 06/30/2025 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Portfolios focused on a single state are subject to greater risk of adverse economic and regulatory changes in that state than a geographically diversified fund. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or financial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. Low- rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. An investor may be subject to the federal Alternative Minimum Tax, and state and local taxes may apply. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a broad measure of the municipal bond market with maturities of at least one year. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Exempt Index is a market value-weighted index of California investment-grade fixed-rate municipal bonds with maturities of one year or more. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes. The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 03/31/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 5 and fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 4 and 5 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Advisor Class and Class A shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

data.title: Marvell Likely Lost A Major Design Win (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It has been just over a couple of weeks since I issued a cautious Buy rating on leading custom silicon player Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL).

My rationale for calling for caution in Marvell’s outlook stemmed from management’s lack of confidence in quantifying strength in the company’s custom silicon program or ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) business. With hyperscalers such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Alphabet’s Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) allocating higher capex dollars towards their own custom GPUs, the custom silicon market continues to become competitive.

At the same time, my evaluation suggested the pessimism surrounding Marvell was elevated, leading me to issue a Buy rating at the time.

However, on Tuesday this week, Marvell announced that it is indefinitely postponing its Investor Day, originally scheduled for June 10. This news only raises the speculation surrounding the design wins it was supposed to share with investors next month.

Marvell’s Investor Day rug-pull is going to have consequences for a few quarters, and the risk/reward profile for the company does not look as attractive to me as it did last month.

I am downgrading the stock to a Hold.

Is Competition Hitting Marvell Where It Hurts?

On the Tuesday announcement, Marvell stated they were postponing their Investor Day to a future date next year:

“In light of the dynamic macroeconomic environment, the company has decided to postpone its previously scheduled Investor Day from June 10, 2025, to a future date in calendar 2026.”

In addition, the company reaffirmed their Q1 revenue targets of $1.875B by narrowing the guidance range from +/-5% to +/-2%.

Additionally, Marvell will be holding a webinar about “the rapidly evolving AI technology landscape” while discussing “the growing opportunity for custom silicon.”

The most important piece I believe investors should focus on is how Marvell framed the indefinite postponement of their Investor Day “to a future date in calendar 2026.”

Let me explain.

In my view, Marvell cancelling the Investor Day suggests that the company may have lost out on major custom silicon design win(s). Marvell typically uses its Investor Day presentations to announce key design wins, like it did last year.

At Marvell’s Q4 earnings call in March, management kept reiterating that they expect to “very significantly exceed” their $2.5 billion AI revenue target this year. When analysts pressed management on learning more about the potential to score more design wins, management was hopeful to share more details on the upcoming Investor Day, which is now cancelled.

In my previous analysis from last month, I explained my calculations as to why I believed Marvell would possibly end up revising their FY26 AI revenue targets to ~$3.1-$3.2B, which would be a ~20% revision above their original FY26 AI revenue targets of $2.5B. Even if Marvell actually ended up upgrading their FY26 AI revenue targets, it would still be under the consensus estimates of $3.5B. And this was still under the $4B target I was originally hoping to hear from Marvell’s management.

Between March this year and the June 10th date (when the Investor Day was supposed to take place), I suspect Marvell’s management was hoping to score a design win and announce that at the event.

But with no design wins likely in the immediate horizon, Marvell cancelled its Investor Day.

It’s also highly likely that Marvell may have lost out on one of the Amazon Trainium ASIC programs to other silicon vendors such as Alchip or Amazon’s own Annapurna Labs. Wall Street analysts are also waking up to the possibility that Marvell may have lost out on Amazon’s Trainium project.

At the same time, industry leader Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) has been scoring wins outside the usual hyperscaler cohort of customers by winning design wins from Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and OpenAI. These developments certainly look like Marvell is getting sandwiched in peer pressure from competitive forces at the top of the market and from smaller players like Alchip.

Plus, I also previously noted how Marvell’s AI networking business is also likely facing pressure from smaller peers like Astera Labs (NASDAQ:ALAB).

All of this pressure will be felt on Marvell’s Data Center business, which I expected to grow at a 50-60% growth rate, per my calculations in my previous post. But with every other business segment of Marvell not showing any conclusive signs of recovery, the growth outlook seems cloudy.

Marvell’s Valuation Is Clouded With Deteriorating Risk/Reward

I am maintaining my expectations on Marvell to grow overall revenues by a range of 35-45% but it is now more weighted towards the lower end of that range. For a growth range in the high thirties, Marvell currently trades at a valuation of ~7x sales on an EV/sales basis.

Industry leader, Broadcom trades at a sales multiple of 15.3x for NTM sales expectations of ~65.2B growing at a near-20% pace.

On a comparison basis, Marvell’s sales multiple has room for mid-single digit expansion implying 3-4% upside in Marvell’s stock or a price target of ~$58.30.

That is not meaningful upside for investors to contend with, especially with the uncertainty in Marvell’s outlook at elevated levels.

Other Factors To Note

While I believe Marvell’s likely inability to score design wins would be a major reason for the company to cancel its Investor Day, it’s also highly likely that Marvell may be facing elevated scrutiny in its business due to its revenue exposure to China.

Per Marvell’s financial reports, 43% of its revenues come from China because of the geographical exposure of its shipments from that region.

With the heightened scrutiny these days on chip shipments in and out of China, it is likely that Marvell may face pressure due to its exposure to China. Trump 2.0 has verbally announced they will revoke the Biden-era AI Diffusion rule, but it is very much possible that new export restrictions may fall in the place of the AI Diffusion rule. This also could be one of the other reasons why Marvell's management pushed out its Investor Day.

While I am staying mid-term cautious on Marvell, I still believe management should be able to find a way to source more custom silicon programs since this is a fast-growing market. Marvell has a wide portfolio of chip products fabricated on the leading edge and is leading the transition from 3nm to 2nm on the ASIC side. I think it is only a matter of time until Marvell announces some breakthrough wins with its customers.

Note: Marvell still has a webinar planned for Tuesday, June 17, 2025, at 10am, which may cover topics such as updates on Marvell’s opportunity in the rapidly evolving AI landscape. This may, at best, involve discussions around market share, but specifics around its AI revenue targets are highly unlikely to be divulged.

Takeaway

For now, the outlook on Marvell’s growth potential looks marginally concerning. It is highly likely that smaller/regional ASIC players have won custom chip programs away from Marvell, while, at the top, industry leader Broadcom forges ahead.

With Marvell sandwiched in between the fierce battle for share in the custom silicon market, the company will see pressure on its valuation multiple. Plus, its exposure to China is also another concern.

For now, I am downgrading Marvell to a Hold.

data.title: Fiverr: Watch Out For Client Drain Amid Strong Near-Term Trends
data.description:
Amid a very uneven macroeconomic backdrop, investors have shown tremendous relief when companies have lifted or even maintained their full-year outlooks during the Q1 earnings season - even when the guidance boost is only by a speck.

Fiverr (NYSE:FVRR), the freelance marketplace platform, is being rewarded handsomely for a relative minor guidance boost. The stock rallied ~5% after posting Q1 results and upping its outlook, helping to reverse a good chunk of the stock's YTD losses and continuing a ~30%+ rally over the past year.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Fiverr in April, when the stock was still trading in the mid-$20s. Since then, Fiverr has glided higher on strong Q1 results, while the company has also executed well with accelerating growth trends. That being said, I still believe the longer-term future and Fiverr's interoperability with AI is in question, leading me to reiterate my neutral rating on Fiverr - especially at its higher valuation.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull/bear case for Fiverr. On the bright side for this company:

  • Fiverr is successfully pushing upmarket. Its mix of business clientele continues to grow, as evidenced by the company's consistent increase in an average spend per buyer. A more prominent mix of enterprise customers can help Fiverr to upsell more effectively and potentially increase order frequency.
  • Near-term incremental revenue from Fiverr Go. The company released its seller-facing Fiverr Go earlier this year, which is an AI tool that helps sellers to build "first drafts" of their projects using AI. Fiverr is drawing additional subscription revenue from bundling Fiverr Go alongside its Seller Plus subscriptions (it is also available a-la-carte), while presumably also appealing to buyers as well for lower-priced AI projects). Fiverr Go also differentiates the company from its main rival Upwork (UPWK), whose Uma AI solution is more of a copilot than a true generative AI bot.

At the same time, however, we have to be wary of the following factors:

  • In the long run, AI is likely to be a net negative for Fiverr. Fiverr Go certainly helps to monetize AI in the short term, but in the long run I fear that many of the basic functions that Fiverr supports (copywriting, web and image design, transcription and translation, etcetera) can be fully automated by clients themselves, without using a seller as an intermediary. Fiverr's business clientele will certainly have the resources to deploy more automation to self-serve the needs that they once outsourced to outside contractors.
  • Declining customer base. Fiverr keeps losing active clients, and amid tougher macroeconomic conditions amid a tariff crunch, it may continue to show weak retention.

From a valuation perspective, Fiverr is trading at a fair price relative to its risks: but neither is its price appealing enough to convince me to rush in. At current share prices near $29, Fiverr trades at a market cap of $1.01 billion. After we net off the $571.8 million of cash and investments against $458.5 million of convertible debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Fiverr's resulting enterprise value is $896.7 million.

For FY25, Fiverr has slightly increased its guidance to $425-$438 million in revenue, or 9-12% y/y growth - lifting only the low end of its range from a prior $422 million view, or 8% y/y growth. It also lifted the low end of its adjusted EBITDA range by $2 million, putting the midpoint of $86.0 million in adjusted EBITDA at a 20.0% margin. It's important for us to recognize that Fiverr basically flowed forward its beat against guidance in Q1, while essentially keeping its Q2-Q4 outlook unchanged (though one could make the argument that if Fiverr guided conservatively for Q1, the rest of the year may also have been set conservatively to start with).

This puts Fiverr's valuation multiples at:

  • 2.1x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 10.4x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

A ~10x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple is certainly cheap enough for a company with double-digit revenue growth and 20%+ adjusted EBITDA growth: but it also reflects the longer-term question marks on Fiverr's business, namely the question of its survivability as AI becomes more broadly proliferated in consumer hands. For now, I recommend staying on the sidelines.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Fiverr's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Fiverr's revenue grew 15% y/y to $107.2 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $106.1 million (+13% y/y) while also accelerating two points from Q4's 13% y/y growth rate. It's notable that Fiverr managed to accelerate its growth in a quarter where many companies, particularly those exposed to weaker business buying conditions, have reported significant macro headwinds as impacting their trajectories.

Fiverr's improvement in growth rates suggests that Fiverr Go, which was launched via a product event in February, has been incremental to Fiverr's top line so far. The company notes that early indicators show sellers have gravitated toward the offering, which has improved their conversion rates and response times to their buyers. On top of this, so far Fiverr management believes there has been no macro impact on its results.

Per CEO Micha Kaufman's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

The past few months have been quite dynamic on the macro front. In the world where we operate, however the overall demand for freelancing has been stable. Our business has no direct exposure to tariffs, and we believe that Fiverr’s compelling value proposition, offering a competitive edge, speed, agility, and cost-effectiveness, may become even more attractive as businesses navigate an evolving economic climate [...]

On Fiverr Go, following the landmark launch event in February, we’ve consistently seen strong engagement among both sellers and buyers. Over 6,000 top-quality sellers on Fiverr have activated Fiverr Go, and over 200,000 buyers have interacted with the product. While still early, initial signals around conversion have been highly encouraging. For sellers who activated Personal Assistant, we saw one-hour conversion uplift of 56% and 14-day conversion uplift of 10% compared to their historical average. This means that because Personal Assistant is always on, and can respond to buyers immediately, with the ability to reference seller experience, suggest pricing, and ultimately close deals, it allows sellers to convert meaningfully better and faster."

Where we continue to see weakness, however, is in Fiverr's active buyer counts. Active buyers at the end of Q1 tallied up to 3.54 million, which was a loss of 94k buyers sequentially since Q4 and a reduction of -11% y/y.

Fiverr defends its declining buyer base as driven by its push upmarket toward enterprise clients, and indeed the ~9% growth in average spend per buyer as well as the company's major leaps in services revenue (via new seller offerings like Fiverr Go) have been able to offset the decline in marketplace buyers. But in the long run, the health of Fiverr's marketplace ultimately depends on buyer activity: without which sellers will not pay for Seller Plus or Fiverr Go.

On the profitability front, Fiverr grew its adjusted EBITDA by 21% y/y to $19.4 million, hitting an 18.1% margin, an improvement of 100bps y/y.

In planning to hit a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year, the company notes that it expects to gain marketing leverage throughout the year while also growing R&D spend at a much slower pace than revenue, with the major product release (Fiverr Go) having already been completed earlier this year. The company has also maintained its medium-term goal of hitting a 25% adjusted EBITDA margin by FY27.

Key takeaways

There is little doubt that Fiverr is executing admirably in a very choppy environment that has kneecapped other tech stocks, but I continue to be very jittery on Fiverr's longer-term prospects, as its continued losses in active buyers and outsized risk of irrelevance from AI cloud the company's growth trajectory. Steer clear here for now, and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Arm Holdings Q4: The Massive Post-Earnings Stock Drop Appears Exaggerated
data.description:
In the runup to the earnings release for the fourth quarter (Q4) of its Fiscal Year 2025 (FY 25), British semiconductor major Arm Holdings plc (NASDAQ:ARM) was expected to show solid growth. The Q4 earnings report released on the 7th of May, in fact, performed as expected: relative to the consensus estimate on LSEG, the company earned revenues of $1.24 billion versus an expectation of $1.23 billion and earnings per share (adjusted) of $0.55 cents versus $0.52.

Arm Holdings holds a deeply-entrenched niche within the semiconductor space: it owns the intellectual property (IP) for the fundamental architecture upon which chips by the likes of Qualcomm (QCOM) and Nvidia (NVDA) are built who, in turn, pay the company royalty fees upon each sale they make. The company also asserts that 99% of all premium smartphones in the world today are powered by its technology.

Simply put: it's essentially indispensable and barriers to entry within its realm are high and driven by long-standing contracts and agreements.

Despite all that and earnings that met expectations, the company's stock slid following the earnings release. Given the valuation already imputed in the stock, it could slide and/or be very rocky territory going forward.

Trend Drilldown

In February this year, the article about Arm's Q3 earnings made some estimates on trends for its FY 25. A number of key estimates were met handily:

In the trend analysis for Q3 earnings, it was estimated that total revenue would grow by 15% in FY 25; actual revenue grew by 24%. Net income was trending to grow by 153% for FY 25; actual net income grew by a massive 159%.

In FY 24, operating income witnessed a massive dip on the back of massive spending on research and development (R&D). As of Q3, operating income was trending to grow by 406%; in actuality, it grew a massive 649%. It can be expected that the company is ploughing a lot more into R&D going forward as well.

A slight uptick in R&D share of operating expenses was registered in FY 25 relative to FY 24 and is likely to continue to hold a high share, regardless of revenue increases.

As estimated in the previous article, operating margin sees a 600% improvement over the previous FY and brought it to the 20%+ levels. Meanwhile net profit margin stayed in the 20% level achieved by Q3. In the final quarter of its FY 25, “related parties” — which include the likes of Apple (AAPL), to whom they supply software services — slightly dipped in revenue contribution while chipmakers—who constitute the bulk of its “external customers” — slightly upped their contribution.

So Why the Drop?

The first factor behind the drop in the stock is two-fold. In the course of the earnings call:

  • Arm forecast Q1 2026 revenue in the $1.0-$1.10 billion range, with a midpoint below analysts' average estimate of $1.10 billion. Also, forecasted were adjusted profits of $0.3-$0.38 cents per share, which was below the estimate of $0.42 cents per share.
  • Arm traditionally provides full-year guidance at the conclusion of every FY release. This time, however, CFO Jason Child said, "Given the uncertainty of the global trade and economic picture, we have lower visibility than is traditional to start the year. As a result, we do not consider it prudent to issue full-year guidance."

CEO Rene Haas stated that the below-expectations guidance is due to a large licensing deal that may not close during Q1 2026. Juxtaposing current events with the language employed, it's likely that this prospective deal was with a party with ties to the People's Republic of China.

Arm's near-omnipresent status in the semiconductor space is predicated on forming relationships with chipmakers all over the world. In the present day, forming relationships in China is a widely-accepted and Wall Street analyst-heralded positive signal for any company, let alone a company like Arm that is square within a sector the Chinese government is spinning money to build out.

While political reactions to President Trump's tariff war against practically every U.S. trading partner are divided along parties, China is largely a matter of bipartisan consensus. In 2017, the United States Trade Representative estimated the cost of Chinese IP infringement to the U.S. economy at $225–$600 billion yearly. In a 2019 CNBC survey, 1 in 5 U.S. corporations stated China has stolen IP within the previous year, while 1 in 3 said it had happened some times during the previous century. In 2022, a Chinese state-backed hacker group was accused of stealing trillions of dollars worth of IP from 30 multinational corporations.

Given that targeted measures to limit theft and calls to the Chinese government to halt the theft failed or fell on deaf ears, both the previous administration as well as the current administration brought into place actions to limit China's access to technology. The current tariff war — at least as it relates to China — will likely continue to resonate for years to come.

For a company like Arm, that specializes in holding IP, the long arm of trade barriers will likely fall heavy.

The second factor is the valuation of the ticker itself, which is wildly high.

The semiconductor industry, on a weighted-average basis, has a Price-to-Earnings (PE) Ratio that is a little over a sixth and Price-to-Earnings-to Growth Ratio that is a little under half of the ticker's. The higher the valuation, the more volatile the ticker.

In Conclusion

The Wall Street analysts' consideration of China's impact might be due for a substantial re-examination. A prime and recent example of this lies in Apple: in the latest earnings analysis article, it was determined that Apple's long-prevalent loss of sales in China (likely on account of economic headwinds affecting consumers) was more than made up for by sales growth in other regions. Likewise, in the semiconductor sector, there are numerous other regions with growing industries wherein the sort of issues implied on China do not exist, have substantial IP protection, and an overall better relationship with the U.S. and other nations. If the prospect of a China loss is the motivating factor behind the analyst-driven downward outlook on the ticker, then this is highly premature: chip demand in the rest of the world is quite stable and innovations in design (again, a cornerstone of Arm's offering) will likely continue to be well-received.

On the other hand, the high P/E and PEG Ratio values indicate that the stock has been heavily overbought. There are always grounds for a correction in this scenario. But this is entirely different from the prospects on the company: while management is reluctant to estimate potential impacts going forward, it is (again) nearly-omnipresent — at least for now.

Despite the downturn in price, investors currently holding the ticker likely should find no cause to divest although diversifying into other sectors is always a good idea. On the other hand, investors of a more conservative bent of mind might take heed of the stock valuation concerns and management's caution and not initiate a position in the ticker (if not already invested). It entirely depends on perspective.

All in all, a "Hold" is in order.

data.title: Invesco Diversified Dividend Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you

may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. Index source: RIMES Technologies Corp. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Investment objective

The fund seeks long-term growth of capital and, secondarily, current income.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M): 10,464.98

Portfolio managers: Peter Santoro, Caroline Le Feuvre, Christopher McMeans, Craig Leopold

Manager perspective and outlook

  • 2025 started with positive expectations for the US economy and markets, but sentiment appeared to reverse by mid-February.
  • Concerns seemed to arise as ambiguity about Trump administration tariff policies appeared to spark fears of a prolonged trade war and possible recession.
  • Forecasts for 2025 US GDP growth appeared to dim considerably amid tariff uncertainty.
  • Reflecting growing concerns about the economy, the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment index fell to its lowest level since November 2022.
  • Inflation appeared to stabilize somewhat but remained higher than the US Federal Reserve’s (Fed) 2% target. Unemployment stayed low, but employment data showed signs of softening.
  • Against this backdrop, the Fed noted that “inflation remains somewhat elevated” and held benchmark interest rates steady, with no cuts expected until mid-2025.
  • We believe the fund is well-positioned to defend against the downside if market and geopolitical uncertainty leads to continued volatility in 2025 and should also be well-positioned to capture a good portion of the upside if markets move higher.
  • Additionally, we believe investors will place a greater focus on dividend paying stocks in 2025 and beyond. In the past, dividends have accounted for a much larger portion of total return than they have over the last decade. However, if we enter a period with more normal returns, dividends could make up a much larger portion of total return.

As of 03/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

Portfolio positioning

Compared to the Russell 1000 Value Index, the fund is generally balanced across both sectors and industries, with exposure to areas that have been benefiting from long-term secular growth tailwinds, including e-commerce, electric vehicles, cloud computing, industrial automation, medical technology and broadband. Our dual focus on companies generating sustainable levels of free cash flow and having healthy balance sheets gives the fund a quality bias with defensive characteristics that we believe should prove valuable if volatility persists in 2025.

Notable Additions

Procter & Gamble (PG) is a multinational consumer goods corporation with a diverse portfolio of personal health, personal care and hygiene products. In our view, the company has reliably good fundamentals with fewer headwinds than food & beverage staples companies, which we believe offers potential for upside and solid downside protection.

Texas Instruments (TXN) is a leading semiconductor company. We see potential for stock outperformance as a cyclical recovery unfolds, driving attractive returns from capacity expansion and renewed confidence in its business model.

Bank of America (BAC) stock is, in our view, attractively valued due to recent market rotation and technical selling. We believe it presents an attractive entry point for a quality money center with a resilient revenue mix.

Eaton’s (ETN) recent sharp stock price pullback created, in our view, an attractive opportunity to initiate a position.

Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) is a leading pharmaceutical products maker. We believe earnings revisions have hit an inflection point as estimates bottomed in 2024. Core products and new product launches also appear to be driving momentum in 2025.

Notable Sales

Analog Devices (ADI) was sold due to concerns about ongoing weakness in industrial end markets as inventory levels correct and lack of visibility for when this might improve.

CME Group (CME) was sold due to concerns about rising downside risks. We also believe there is potential for anti-trust issues to arise, which could negatively affect CME.

Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY) was sold because the stock had performed well and was approaching our price target. We also had concerns about several emerging headline risks that might have a disproportionate effect on the stock.

ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) was sold and we used the proceeds to initiate a position in Eaton. A stock price pullback created an opportunity for us to upgrade to a higher quality US-domiciled stock.

American Electric Power (AEP) was sold due to concerns about rising valuation levels.

Performance highlights

The fund delivered a positive return for the quarter but underperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index. Leading drivers of underperformance included stock selection in the IT, financials and materials sectors. An overweight in the IT sector also dampened results amid a broad rotation away from high valuation stocks. Financials stocks were weaker during the quarter as the risk of loan defaults increased due to pressure on consumer spending. Conversely, stock selection in the health care, industrials, consumer staples and consumer discretionary sectors contributed the most to relative performance. Defensive attributes of these sectors helped stabilize results as investor sentiment appeared to sour amid apparently growing tariff policy uncertainty.

Contributors to performance

Philip Morris International delivered quarterly earnings that exceeded analysts’ expectations, with strong sales volumes in its traditional tobacco and smoke-free segments.

Chevron released a quarterly earnings report that fell short of analysts’ estimates, yet its share price ended the period in positive territory. Momentum was fueled by solid annual growth projections and efforts to streamline the business and cut operational costs through divestitures of select assets.

AT&T reported strong quarterly results and reiterated its forward-looking guidance, driving the share price higher. The telecommunication company’s strategy to bundle fiber and wireless offerings has seemingly helped it attract subscribers and retain existing customers.

Detractors from performance

Marvell Technology released earnings and forward-looking guidance that fell short of elevated analyst expectations. Its artificial intelligence (AI)-related businesses have been growing ahead of expectations, but the stock’s aggressive pullback was likely fueled by management’s overly confident tone even as results were more muted than anticipated.

Salesforce delivered a mixed quarterly report. Earnings beat analysts’ expectations, but sales were weaker than expected. Forward-looking sales guidance was also below expected targets, causing a decline in share price.

Broadcom experienced weakness during the quarter along with other AI-related stocks due to ongoing apparent concerns about the effect of tariffs on the broader economy and a possible slowdown in AI-related spending.

Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations. Attribution methodology notes: The attribution provides analysis of the effects of several portfolio management decisions, including allocation and security selection. Securities classified as “Other” may include non-equity securities, derivatives, and securities for which a sector classification may not be appropriate. The portfolio is actively managed and portfolio holdings are subject to change. The percentage weights represented for the portfolio are dollar weighted based on market value. Market allocation effect shows the excess contribution due to sector/market allocation. A positive allocation effect implies that the choice of sector weights in the portfolio added value to the portfolio contribution with respect to the benchmark and vice versa. Selection effect shows the excess contribution due to security selection. A positive selection effect implies that the choice of stocks in the portfolio added value to the portfolio contribution with respect to the benchmark and vice versa. Total effect is the difference in contribution between the

benchmark and portfolio. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Unless otherwise specified, all information is as of 03/31/25. Unless stated otherwise, Index refers to Russell 1000 Value Index (USD).

Asset allocation/diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

The Russell 1000® Value Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of large-cap value stocks. The Russell 1000 Value Index is a trademark/service mark of the Frank Russell Co. Russell® is a trademark of the Frank Russell Co. An investment cannot be made directly in an index.

About Risk

Securities that pay high dividends as a group can fall out of favor with the market, causing such companies to underperform companies that do not pay high dividends. Also changes in the dividend policies of the companies and the capital resources available for such companies' dividend payments may affect the fund.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The Fund's value may be affected by changes in the stock markets. Stock markets may experience significant short-term volatility and may fall or rise sharply at times. Adverse events in any part of the equity or fixed-income markets may have unexpected negative effects on other market segments. Different stock markets may behave differently from each other and U.S. stock markets may move in the opposite direction from one or more foreign stock markets.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

A value style of investing is subject to the risk that the valuations never improve or that the returns will trail other styles of investing or the overall stock markets.

The Fund is subject to certain other risks. Please see the current prospectus for more information regarding the risks associated with an investment in the Fund.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management, are based on current market conditions and are subject to change without notice. These opinions may differ from those of other Invesco investment professionals.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Note: Not all products available at all firms. Financial professionals, please contact your home office.

The fund holdings are organized according to the Global Industry Classification Standard, which was developed by and is the exclusive property and service mark of MSCI Inc. and Standard & Poor’s.

* Alpha (cash adjusted) is a measure of performance on a risk-adjusted basis. Beta (cash adjusted) is a measure of relative risk and the slope of regression. Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund’s range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund’s short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio’s excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund’s financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.

Morningstar

Source: ©2025 Morningstar Inc. All rights reserved. The information contained herein is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers. It may not be copied or distributed and is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Open-end mutual funds and exchange-traded funds are considered a single population for comparison purposes. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed currently or in the past, the ranking would have been lower. Rankings for other share classes may differ due to different performance characteristics.

Before investing, consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. Visit invesco.com/fundprospectus for a prospectus/summary prospectus containing this information. Read it carefully before investing.

Invesco Diversified Dividend Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Forvia: Protecting Itself Against Tariff Impact
data.description:
Introduction

Forvia (OTCPK:FURCF) (OTCPK:FAURY) is a French car parts supplier with a worldwide presence and increasing sales into China. The company has been trying to clean up its act after the acquisition of Hella, but as I mentioned in my previous article, the financial improvements are quite slow. That being said, there's a noticeable improvement, and as the company is rapidly covering its exposure to US tariffs, Forvia is still worth a closer look.

Forvia has its primary listing on Euronext Paris, where it's trading with FRVIA as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is approximately 1.6 million shares per day for a monetary value of around 11M EUR per day. I will use the Euro as the base currency throughout this article as it’s the currency the company reports its financial results in and it’s the currency for its most liquid listing.

Somewhat upbeat after Q1 – but it’s not an easy world

In the first quarter of 2025, Forvia reported a total revenue of 6.7B EUR, an increase of approximately 2.6% of which about 80% came from organic growth initiatives with the remainder coming from currency impact changes. Forvia continues to grow its business with Chinese OEMs at a double-digit rate. China now accounts for about 20% of the company’s reported revenue and remains a very important growth pillar for Forvia.

Unfortunately, the company does not provide detailed financial statements on a quarterly basis, and we’ll have to wait for the H1 report to get a better look at the underlying performance and operations.

That’s why I think it makes sense to quickly look back at the FY 2024 results to get a better understanding of the platform Forvia continues to build on.

In 2024, the company reported a total revenue of just under 27B EUR, which was a 1% decrease compared to the preceding year. Total operating income after amortizing acquired intangible assets fell by approximately 3% to 1.21B EUR, and after recording some additional non-recurring expenses, the pre-tax income was just 229M EUR on a reported basis, which resulted in a net loss of 24M EUR. That being said, there was a 161M EUR profit that was attributable to the non-controlling interests, which means the net loss attributable to the Forvia shareholders was roughly 185M EUR.

While that definitely doesn’t sound great, keep in mind there are quite a few non-recurring items that weighed on the reported results with, for instance, a 362M EUR hit from expenses related to the reorganization of the corporate structure and a 84M EUR "other" hit including litigation settlements.

The company also publishes its "net cash flow calculation" and that’s an interesting one. As you can see below, the total net cash flow in FY 2024 was 655M EUR, but this includes 619M EUR in working capital contributions and 208M EUR in cash restructuring expenses.

If I’d filter out those two items, the underlying adjusted net cash flow was approximately 245M EUR or 1.24 EUR per share.

The guidance was reconfirmed despite the impact of tariffs

So despite the uncertainty when it comes to international trade, Forvia has reconfirmed its full-year guidance after a successful start of the year.

The company now expects a total revenue of 26.3-2.7.5B EUR and an operating margin of 5.2-6% of the revenue. Meanwhile, the balance sheet should continue to get stronger as the company now expects to have a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of less than 1.8 by the end of this year, and this should decrease further to 1.5 by the end of net year thanks to the impact of a positive free cash flow, improving margins and selling off some assets.

The company has also provided an update on how it plans and expects to navigate through the current, unpredictable climate. Forvia uses local networks of industrial operations and suppliers in North America, and most of the sales that currently find place between Mexico and the US are already subject to duties. And for the US-based purchases of components outside of the US, according to Forvia, in excess of 80% of those are eligible for USMCA qualification.

Forvia is also already in discussions with its clients to get them to sign pass-through agreements. The company mentioned it has already reached agreements for almost 50% of its residual exposure, and it aims to fully cover its exposure.

Should new tariffs be enacted, Forvia will deploy a similar approach and secure pass-through agreements.

Investment thesis

Forvia’s share price has now been in a pretty continuous decline for about four years now, and although the company is working hard to turn the tide, the market is now a bit wary of suppliers to the automotive industry until it sees how all the tariff talk actually plays out.

Forvia is actually delivering on its expectations: The margins are improving, and the balance sheet is getting stronger. The equity is currently valued at less than 0.5 times the adjusted EBITDA, and the enterprise value right now represents less than 2.5 times the adjusted EBITDA. This makes Forvia cheap on pretty much any metric you look at, and I hope the company will also start to report decent earnings and free cash flow, so the market can start to appreciate Forvia a bit more.

I had written out of the money put options on Forvia before, and those expired out of the money. I have now started to write put options again, with the first batch having exercise prices at 6.2 and 6.4 EUR. I'm trying to write more put options, but I’m in no rush and prefer to get a good price more than a fast fill.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
